content.text: Retraining closes the ultimate feedback loop: production failures feed into new training data, producing a better model that ships back to production. Unlike prompt engineering or RAG tuning — which improve LLM responses without touching model weights — retraining updates the model's parameters, enabling improvements that cannot be achieved through inference-time techniques alone. Done well, each retraining run makes the model measurably better at exactly the tasks where it has been failing real users.

attrs.alt: Retraining pipeline: data collection, trigger conditions, training method selection, evaluation gate, and canary deployment

content.text: Retraining has a non-trivial cost in GPU time, engineer attention, and deployment risk. Trigger it on clear signals, not on a fixed schedule:
Clear quality regression; investigate before training
Regression confirmed by automated eval suite
Red-team finding; treat as incident before training
Keep model current with evolving user patterns
Never retrain in response to a single incident without first investigating whether a prompt change, system prompt fix, or retrieval improvement can resolve the issue faster and cheaper.
Choose the training method based on the size of the behavioural delta required and the available data:

LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) adds trainable rank-decomposition matrices to the attention layers while keeping base model weights frozen. It requires 1–5% of the compute of full fine-tuning and is the right choice for incremental improvements from feedback data:

# LoRA fine-tuning with Hugging Face PEFT + TRL
from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model, TaskType
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer, TrainingArguments
from trl import SFTTrainer
from datasets import load_dataset

MODEL_ID = "meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct"
OUTPUT_DIR = "./checkpoints/lora-feedback-v1"

lora_config = LoraConfig(
    task_type=TaskType.CAUSAL_LM,
    r=16,                    # rank — higher = more capacity
    lora_alpha=32,           # scaling factor = alpha/r = 2
    lora_dropout=0.05,
    target_modules=["q_proj", "v_proj", "k_proj", "o_proj"],
    bias="none",
)

model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    MODEL_ID, torch_dtype="bfloat16", device_map="auto"
)
model = get_peft_model(model, lora_config)
model.print_trainable_parameters()
# trainable params: 41,943,040 || all params: 8,072,204,288 || trainable%: 0.52

training_args = TrainingArguments(
    output_dir=OUTPUT_DIR,
    num_train_epochs=3,
    per_device_train_batch_size=4,
    gradient_accumulation_steps=8,
    learning_rate=2e-4,
    bf16=True,
    logging_steps=10,
    save_strategy="steps",
    save_steps=100,
    warmup_ratio=0.03,
    lr_scheduler_type="cosine",
)

dataset = load_dataset("json", data_files="train_cases.jsonl")["train"]

trainer = SFTTrainer(
    model=model,
    args=training_args,
    train_dataset=dataset,
    tokenizer=AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(MODEL_ID),
)
trainer.train()
QLoRA extends LoRA by quantising the frozen base to 4-bit NF4, enabling 70B-parameter fine-tuning on a single 80 GB GPU. Use

Direct Preference Optimisation trains on (prompt, chosen, rejected) triples — naturally produced when users regenerate a response and then approve the new one. DPO updates the model to prefer chosen responses over rejected ones without a separate reward model:

# DPO fine-tuning from preference pairs
from trl import DPOTrainer, DPOConfig
from peft import LoraConfig
from datasets import Dataset

# Dataset format for DPO: prompt, chosen, rejected
# {"prompt": "...", "chosen": "...", "rejected": "..."}

dpo_config = DPOConfig(
    output_dir="./checkpoints/dpo-preference-v1",
    num_train_epochs=1,          # DPO is sample-efficient; 1 epoch usually enough
    per_device_train_batch_size=2,
    gradient_accumulation_steps=16,
    learning_rate=5e-7,          # much lower LR than SFT to avoid over-optimising
    beta=0.1,                    # KL-divergence penalty strength
    bf16=True,
    loss_type="sigmoid",         # standard DPO loss
)

peft_config = LoraConfig(
    r=8, lora_alpha=16,
    target_modules=["q_proj", "v_proj"],
    task_type="CAUSAL_LM",
)

trainer = DPOTrainer(
    model=model,
    ref_model=None,     # set to None when using PEFT (ref policy inferred from adapter)
    args=dpo_config,
    train_dataset=preference_dataset,
    peft_config=peft_config,
    tokenizer=tokenizer,
)
trainer.train()

parameter (default 0.1) controls how tightly the model is constrained to the reference policy. A higher beta (0.2–0.5) prevents reward hacking at the cost of slower adaptation. Use 1 epoch: DPO overfits easily.

A critical failure mode in continual retraining is catastrophic forgetting: the model improves on new feedback cases but degrades on capabilities it had before. Mitigate it with experience replay — mixing new data with random samples from historical training:

# Compute training data mix to avoid catastrophic forgetting
import random, json
from pathlib import Path

def build_training_mix(
    feedback_cases: list[dict],    # new feedback-derived cases
    replay_ratio: float = 0.3,     # 30% replay data to prevent forgetting
    replay_pool_path: str = "replay_pool.jsonl",
) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Mix new feedback data with replay samples from historical training data.
    The replay prevents the model forgetting skills it had before this run.
    """
    n_new = len(feedback_cases)
    n_replay = int(n_new * replay_ratio / (1 - replay_ratio))

    replay_pool = [json.loads(l) for l in Path(replay_pool_path).read_text().splitlines()]
    replay_sample = random.sample(replay_pool, min(n_replay, len(replay_pool)))

    mixed = feedback_cases + replay_sample
    random.shuffle(mixed)
    print(f"Training mix: {n_new} new + {len(replay_sample)} replay = {len(mixed)} total")
    return mixed

# Usage
# training_data = build_training_mix(new_feedback_cases, replay_ratio=0.3)

A 70/30 new-to-replay ratio is a reasonable starting point. Monitor replay-task performance in your eval suite — if replay-task scores drop, increase the replay ratio. For major behaviour changes (new domain, new language), move to a fresh SFT run on the full combined dataset rather than replay mixing.
Every trained checkpoint must be registered with metadata before deployment. The registry serves as the audit trail connecting each production model to its training data, trigger, and eval scores:

# Register a trained model checkpoint in the model registry
# Using MLflow as the registry backend
import mlflow
import json

def register_model(
    checkpoint_path: str,
    training_run_id: str,
    eval_scores: dict,
    data_snapshot_id: str,
    model_name: str = "llm-production",
) -> str:
    with mlflow.start_run(run_id=training_run_id):
        # Log eval metrics
        mlflow.log_metrics(eval_scores)
        mlflow.log_param("data_snapshot_id", data_snapshot_id)

        # Log model artifacts
        mlflow.log_artifacts(checkpoint_path, artifact_path="model")

        # Register in model registry
        model_uri = f"runs:/{training_run_id}/model"
        registered = mlflow.register_model(model_uri, model_name)
        version = registered.version

        # Tag the version with pass/fail gate status
        gate_passed = all(
            eval_scores.get(k, 0) >= v
            for k, v in {"safety_score": 0.95, "eval_pass_rate": 0.85}.items()
        )
        mlflow.MlflowClient().set_model_version_tag(
            model_name, version, "gate_status",
            "passed" if gate_passed else "failed"
        )

    print(f"Registered {model_name} v{version} (gate={'passed' if gate_passed else 'failed'})")
    return version
Tag every checkpoint with its gate status at registration time. Deployment tooling should refuse to promote a checkpoint tagged
to production without explicit human override.
Never deploy a new model version to 100% of traffic immediately. Use a staged canary rollout with feedback-rate monitoring at each stage:

# Canary deployment controller — gradual traffic ramp
import time

CANARY_STAGES = [
    {"pct": 1,   "soak_hours": 24, "neg_rate_max": 0.10},
    {"pct": 10,  "soak_hours": 24, "neg_rate_max": 0.09},
    {"pct": 50,  "soak_hours": 12, "neg_rate_max": 0.08},
    {"pct": 100, "soak_hours": 0,  "neg_rate_max": None},
]

def run_canary(model_version: str, traffic_router):
    for stage in CANARY_STAGES:
        pct = stage["pct"]
        traffic_router.set_canary_pct(model_version, pct)
        print(f"Canary at {pct}% — soaking for {stage['soak_hours']}h")

        if stage["soak_hours"] > 0:
            time.sleep(stage["soak_hours"] * 3600)
            neg_rate = get_neg_rate(model_version, hours=stage["soak_hours"])
            if neg_rate > stage["neg_rate_max"]:
                traffic_router.set_canary_pct(model_version, 0)
                raise RuntimeError(
                    f"Canary aborted at {pct}%: neg_rate={neg_rate:.1%} > {stage['neg_rate_max']:.1%}"
                )

    print(f"Canary complete — {model_version} fully promoted")

The soak period at 1% traffic is the most important: it exposes the new model to enough real queries to detect safety or quality regressions before they affect most users. At 1% of 1 million daily queries, you still get 10 000 samples in 24 hours — enough to detect a 1% absolute change in negative rate with high confidence.

Feedback data is biased toward edge cases and failure modes. If more than 40% of training data comes from feedback negatives, the model may optimise for those patterns at the expense of common-case quality. The replay ratio is your primary lever.
Never include feedback cases that are in the eval set in the training data. Keep a strict separation: when a feedback case is added to the eval set, mark it ineligible for training.

SFT data from human reviewers tends to be longer and more detailed than the model's natural outputs. Fine-tuning on it can cause the model to produce longer responses even when brevity is better. Monitor mean output length per category.

Any fine-tuning run can inadvertently weaken safety alignment. Run the full safety red-team eval on every candidate checkpoint, even when the training data was benign. Make safety gate failure a hard block.

Before any canary, run the candidate model on a sample of live requests without returning responses to users (shadow mode). This surfaces obvious failures in latency, format, or error rate before any user impact.

Retraining converts production feedback into model improvements. Trigger it on data signals (negative rate, eval regressions, feedback batch size) rather than arbitrary schedules. Choose LoRA for incremental improvements, DPO for preference alignment from regenerate signals, and full SFT for major capability changes. Mix new data with historical replay at 70/30 to prevent catastrophic forgetting. Register every checkpoint with eval scores and gate status, and deploy via staged canary rollout with automatic rollback on negative-rate spikes.
